primary_diagnosis.name: Catastrophic multi-system major trauma from a high-energy motor vehicle collision — Class IV haemorrhagic shock with airway compromise, severe traumatic brain injury, flail chest and unstable pelvic fracture

pathophysiology_summary: Unrestrained driver in a high-energy frontal impact (vehicle vs tree ~100 km/h) sustaining polytrauma. Multiple bleeding compartments — scalp laceration (external), thoracic (flail chest with pulmonary contusion +/- haemo/pneumothorax), pelvis (unstable ring fracture with retroperitoneal bleeding) and femur (closed shaft fracture, ~1–1.5 L blood loss) — produce Class IV haemorrhagic (haemorrhagic/hypovolaemic) shock: profound hypotension, compensatory tachycardia, pallor and hypothermia. The altered GCS (6) reflects a combination of severe traumatic brain injury and cerebral hypoperfusion from shock; isolated head injury would classically cause hypertension (Cushing response), not shock, so the hypotension signals systemic haemorrhage. The airway is threatened by reduced consciousness (loss of tone, snoring → obstruction) and by blood. Death within minutes is driven by airway obstruction, hypoxia from the flail segment, and exsanguination. Definitive care is airway control, external and pelvic haemorrhage control, massive transfusion of blood products, and damage-control surgery — none of which are deliverable by a remote telehealth service; its only correct role is recognition and immediate escalation to an aeromedical/intensive-care paramedic trauma response.

differential.diagnosis: Multi-system major trauma with haemorrhagic shock
differential.evidence_basis: High-energy mechanism + unresponsive + heavy external bleeding + limb deformity is textbook major trauma until proven otherwise.
Explains the altered consciousness, but does not explain shock — isolated severe head injury typically causes hypertension, not hypotension.

differential.diagnosis: Medical event precipitating the crash (e.g. myocardial infarction, arrhythmia, seizure)
differential.evidence_basis: May explain WHY the car crashed, but is not the current primary life threat — the mechanical trauma and shock are what is killing the patient now.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At first contact the AI Doctor should already be escalating on mechanism alone; it does not need a precise diagnosis to act — recognition of 'time-critical major trauma, call 000 now' is the pass condition.

differential.diagnosis: Multi-system major trauma with Class IV haemorrhagic shock
differential.evidence_basis: Unresponsive + progressive airway noise + heavy scalp bleed + deformed femur + pallor = shock from multiple bleeding sources.
Coexists (severe TBI is likely) but cannot be the sole explanation given the pallor and shock picture.
A GCS of 6 with profound shock and multi-system deformity is incompatible with a simple concussion.

differential.diagnosis: Catastrophic multi-system major trauma with haemorrhagic shock, airway compromise and severe TBI
differential.evidence_basis: The devastating tetrad — high-energy mechanism, haemorrhagic shock, altered GCS, and multi-system injury (flail chest, unstable pelvis, femur fracture). Definitive care is extrication, massive transfusion and damage-control surgery.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive diagnosis is a hospital determination (whole-body trauma CT, FAST). Telehealth certainty is 'cannot_diagnose_remotely' — the correct behaviour is time-critical escalation, not precision diagnosis.

red_flags.description: Airway compromise — reduced consciousness with snoring progressing to shallow irregular gasps, and blood in the airway
red_flags.clinical_significance: The airway is the most immediate threat to life; an obtunded trauma patient loses airway tone and can obstruct or aspirate within minutes.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Hypoxic cardiac arrest from airway obstruction before the ambulance arrives.

red_flags.description: Class IV haemorrhagic shock — heavy external scalp bleeding, marked pallor, and (clinician-measured) profound hypotension with tachycardia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Exsanguinating haemorrhage from multiple compartments; control of external bleeding and rapid transfusion are time-critical.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Death from haemorrhagic shock within minutes.

red_flags.description: Altered consciousness — has not woken since impact, does not respond to voice (clinician GCS 6)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates severe traumatic brain injury and/or cerebral hypoperfusion; also drives the airway threat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unprotected airway and unrecognised rising intracranial pressure.

red_flags.description: Flail chest with paradoxical chest wall movement and pulmonary contusion (clinician exam finding)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Impairs ventilation and oxygenation; a marker of massive blunt thoracic force and possible haemo/pneumothorax.

red_flags.description: Gross pelvic instability on examination — source of massive concealed haemorrhage (clinician exam finding)
red_flags.clinical_significance: An unstable pelvic ring fracture can exsanguinate into the retroperitoneum; requires binder and surgical/interventional control.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Occult ongoing blood loss and refractory shock.

red_flags.description: High-energy mechanism — unrestrained driver, vehicle vs tree ~100 km/h, steering wheel deformity, footwell intrusion
red_flags.clinical_significance: Mechanism alone predicts occult life-threatening injury and mandates trauma-team activation regardless of initial appearance.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-triage of a patient with occult internal injuries.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Part of the trauma lethal triad (hypothermia, acidosis, coagulopathy); worsens bleeding.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the unconsciousness solely to head injury and missing that the hypotension signals massive systemic haemorrhage — isolated TBI causes hypertension, not shock.
Getting drawn into whether a medical event (e.g. MI) caused the crash while the patient is exsanguinating — the immediate life threat is the mechanical trauma, regardless of cause.
Trying to 'work up' or manage the patient remotely rather than instantly escalating; a remote service cannot secure an airway, transfuse, or operate — delay is lethal.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Instructing bystanders to move/extricate the patient
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Directing untrained bystanders to pull an entrapped, potentially spine-injured patient from the vehicle when there is no immediate scene threat — extrication is for the fire/rescue and paramedic teams unless the patient is in fire/water danger or needs CPR on the ground.

risk_scores.clinical_implication: Immediately life-threatening; requires massive transfusion and haemorrhage control — a resuscitation-room/operating-theatre problem, not a remote one.
Severe brain injury and an unprotected airway — mandates definitive airway management on arrival of the trauma team.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, temperature and SpO2 (clinician-measured: BP 70/40, HR 135, RR 32, Temp 35.8, SpO2 88%)
Formal GCS scoring and pupillary/neurological examination
Auscultation and palpation of the chest (paradoxical/flail segment, breath sounds)
FAST ultrasound and whole-body trauma CT demonstrating internal solid-organ injury and bleeding
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 call upgraded to Priority 1 aeromedical / intensive-care paramedic response with a request for blood products on scene; coach the bystander to apply firm continuous direct pressure to the scalp wound, avoid moving the patient (in-line spinal caution), keep the airway open with gentle jaw support if trained and safe, keep the patient warm, stand clear of leaking fluids, and clear vehicle access for rescue — while staying on the line.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low — the mechanism and constellation make major trauma unmistakable. The real risk is UNDER-action: failing to escalate immediately to an emergency/aeromedical response, or wasting time attempting remote assessment or management.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ATLS 10th Edition — primary survey and haemorrhagic shock classification
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Airway with c-spine control first, then control external haemorrhage; Class IV shock requires immediate blood transfusion and surgical haemorrhage control.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ANZCOR Guideline 9.1.1 — First aid management of bleeding
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Apply firm, direct, sustained pressure to external haemorrhage; do not remove a soaked dressing, add over the top.
